title: Cat Vomiting Undigested Food: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your cat throwing up whole food? Learn why this happens, when it is a medium-level urgency, and how photos help your veterinarian diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Undigested food is usually caused by regurgitation from eating too fast, but you should contact a veterinarian within 24 hours if the behavior is frequent or accompanied by lethargy.

What does it mean when my cat throws up undigested food?

When your cat brings up food that looks exactly like it did in the bowl, it is often a process called regurgitation. Unlike active vomiting, where the body uses abdominal muscles to eject stomach contents, regurgitation is a passive process where the food is expelled from the esophagus or shortly after reaching the stomach. This often occurs because the food was consumed too quickly, or there is an irritation in the upper digestive tract.

Is it an emergency if my cat is regurgitating food?

  • The urgency level for undigested food vomiting is generally Medium.
  • If your cat behaves normally, remains hydrated, and only does this once, it may just be a case of 'scarf and barf' (eating too fast).
  • Contact your veterinarian within the next 12 to 24 hours if the vomiting happens multiple times in 24 hours or if your cat stops eating entirely.
  • Seek medical attention if the vomiting is accompanied by lethargy or other signs of illness.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's regurgitated food for the vet?

  • A photo provides vital clinical data, such as whether the shape is tubular or a puddle, which indicates where the problem is occurring.
  • Veterinarians look for the presence of bile or mucus and the degree of digestion.
  • Showing your vet a photo can help them quickly distinguish between a simple dietary indiscretion and a more serious esophageal or gastric issue.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Vomiting undigested food in cats can stem from dietary indiscretion or intolerance, such as consuming novel, spoiled, or contaminated foods or foreign material. Other causes include drug or toxin ingestion (e.g., antibiotics, NSAIDs, corticosteroids, plants, chemicals), systemic illnesses like pancreatitis, uremic gastropathy, or hypoadrenocorticism, endoparasitism, or bacterial or viral infections. If vomiting persists, or is accompanied by depression, abdominal discomfort, or fever, reevaluation by a veterinarian is warranted. Examination of the oral cavity for foreign material like yarn or needles is important, and abdominal radiographs should be considered to identify radiopaque foreign objects or signs of intestinal obstruction. Chronic vomiting may indicate underlying thyroid, liver, or kidney dysfunction and should be investigated.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 390)

faqs.question: Should I withhold food after my cat vomits?
faqs.answer: Generally, it is helpful to pick up the food for 2 to 4 hours to let the stomach settle, but always ensure they have access to fresh water unless your vet advises otherwise.

faqs.question: Could it be a hairball even if I only see food?
faqs.answer: Yes. A hairball can act as a temporary plug in the stomach, causing the cat to regurgitate any food they try to eat on top of it.

faqs.question: When does vomiting become a critical emergency?
faqs.answer: If you see blood (red or coffee-ground appearance), if the cat is straining and unproductive, or if they appear extremely weak or collapse, seek emergency care immediately.
